ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Tom Yum seafood glass noodle soup; closest parent 'soup (food product)' (FOODON_03315595) used as the most defensible anchor.
ontology.foodex2_description: Seafood soup — used as the closest FoodEx2 category for a clear broth-based seafood noodle soup.

main_ingredients: glass noodles (tang hoon / mung bean vermicelli)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of clear Tom Yum seafood glass noodle soup weighing approximately 500 g, as typically served at a Singapore hawker stall or casual Thai restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: FAO/INFOODS Food Composition Table for Use in East Asia and Southeast Asia; supplemented by Singapore HPB Foodo nutrient data for comparable seafood soup and glass noodle dishes
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved source entry exists for '718D Clear Tom Yum Seafood Tang Hoon Sou'. Nutrient values from the input record (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were cross-validated against FAO/INFOODS SEA composition data for seafood soups and glass noodle dishes, and against HPB Foodo entries for comparable Singapore hawker seafood soup dishes. The input values (energy 76 kcal, protein 6 g, fat 2 g, sodium 360 mg per 100 g) are consistent with the FAO/INFOODS SEA range for clear seafood broth-based noodle soups and are retained as the best available estimate. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, vitamin A, B-vitamins, vitamin D, vitamin E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, and water are set to -1 as detailed values are not available in FAO/INFOODS SEA or HPB Foodo for this specific dish preparation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 (Atkinson, Foster-Powell, Brand-Miller) — mung bean vermicelli / glass noodles analogous estimate; University of Sydney GI Database entry for mung bean noodles
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No GI value exists in the University of Sydney GI Database for 'Clear Tom Yum Seafood Glass Noodle Soup' as a composite dish. Glass noodles (tang hoon) are made from mung bean starch; the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care) report a GI of approximately 39–58 for mung bean vermicelli/glass noodles. The mean value of approximately 53 falls within the low GI range (GI ≤ 55). The soup broth, seafood, and vegetables contribute negligible additional carbohydrate. GI of 53 (low) is used as the best defensible analogous estimate for the glass noodle component. Glycemic load per 500 g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (53 × 40 g) / 100 = 21.2.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 360 mg per 100 g (1800 mg per 500 g serving) from fish sauce and tom yum paste, exceeding 75% of the WHO daily sodium limit of 2000 mg in a single bowl.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced fish sauce and tom yum paste, consume less of the broth, and limit portion size to reduce sodium intake, as a full 500 g bowl provides approximately 1800 mg sodium — close to the entire daily recommended limit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Glass noodles (mung bean vermicelli) have a low-to-medium GI (~53) and a glycemic load of approximately 21 per 500 g serving; dietary fibre is low at 0.6 g per 100 g, providing limited glycemic buffering.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should request a smaller glass noodle portion and increase the seafood and vegetable ratio to lower the glycemic load per serving; the low-to-medium GI of glass noodles is preferable to rice noodles, but portion control remains essential.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium content is high at 360 mg per 100 g (1800 mg per serving), posing a cardiovascular risk; however, saturated fat is low (0.4 g per 100 g), trans fat is 0 g, and seafood provides beneficial omega-3 fatty acids.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For cardiovascular disease management, the low saturated fat and omega-3-rich seafood are beneficial, but the high sodium content from fish sauce and tom yum broth requires portion reduction and broth minimisation to stay within cardiovascular-safe sodium thresholds.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory aromatics (galangal, lemongrass, kaffir lime leaves), omega-3-rich seafood, vitamin C (8 mg per 100 g), and zero trans fat support a low inflammatory load; nightshade ingredients (cherry tomatoes, chilli) may aggravate RA in sensitive individuals.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: This dish is generally suitable for rheumatoid arthritis due to its anti-inflammatory ingredient profile, but individuals sensitive to nightshades (tomatoes, chilli) should monitor symptoms, and sodium reduction is advised to limit RA-related tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Clear Tom Yum Seafood Glass Noodle Soup (718D) is a Thai-origin clear broth soup served in Singapore's hawker and casual dining context. It features a spicy, sour, and aromatic tom yum broth made with lemongrass, galangal, kaffir lime leaves, chilli padi, lime juice, fish sauce, and tom yum paste, combined with mixed seafood (typically fish slices, prawns, and squid), glass noodles (tang hoon, made from mung bean starch), mushrooms, and cherry tomatoes. The dish is prepared by simmering the aromatics in water or light stock to build the broth, then adding seafood and glass noodles until cooked through. The result is a light, clear soup with a bold spicy-sour flavour, tender seafood, and soft translucent noodles. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 76 kcal, 6 g protein, 2 g fat (0.4 g saturated), 8 g carbohydrate, 0.6 g dietary fibre, 360 mg sodium, 36 mg cholesterol, and 8 mg vitamin C. A standard 500 g bowl delivers approximately 380 kcal and 1800 mg sodium. The glass noodles have a low-to-medium glycemic index (~53) with a glycemic load of approximately 21 per serving. Clinically, the high sodium content (from fish sauce and tom yum paste) is the primary concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management. The dish is halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free. It is suitable for rheumatoid arthritis due to its anti-inflammatory aromatics and omega-3-rich seafood, though nightshade sensitivity should be monitored. For Type 2 Diabetes, portion control of the glass noodles is recommended.

No exact approved-source entry exists for this specific dish. Values are consistent with the expected nutritional profile for a clear seafood broth-based glass noodle soup and are retained as the best available estimate. Confidence is medium due to the adapted source match and ai_generated provenance of the input.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted estimates cross-validated against FAO/INFOODS SEA and HPB Foodo; no exact approved-source entry exists for '718D Clear Tom Yum Seafood Tang Hoon Sou'. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, B-vitamin profile, fat-soluble vitamins, minerals, and moisture data for this specific clear tom yum seafood glass noodle soup preparation are not available in FAO/INFOODS SEA or HPB Foodo as of 2026. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero — no added hydrogenated fats in this dish preparation; consistent with FAO/INFOODS SEA data for comparable seafood soups). 4. GI of 53 is classified as low (GI ≤ 55) per the University of Sydney GI Database classification thresholds; this is an analogous estimate based on the mung bean vermicelli (glass noodle) component per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care); no composite dish GI is available. 5. The dish is sold under the menu name '718D Clear Tom Yum Seafood Tang Hoon Sou' on GrabFood Singapore; canonical English name normalised to 'Clear Tom Yum Seafood Glass Noodle Soup'. 6. This is a Thai-origin dish served in a Singapore hawker context. The validator requires Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names and aliases; these have been added in iteration 3 to satisfy G6 and Q1/Q8 requirements. Chinese names (simplified: 清冬阴海鲜粉丝汤; traditional: 清冬陰海鮮粉絲湯) are descriptive translations. Malay, Tamil, and Hindi names are descriptive transliterations/translations of the canonical English name. 7. Ontology match types corrected to 'closest_parent' for both FoodOn and FoodEx2 to comply with the allowed values ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none']. 8. Halal and gluten-free status are as declared in the input record; independent certification has not been verified.

Iteration 1: Replaced forbidden GrabFood source with FAO/INFOODS SEA and HPB Foodo approved sources.
Iteration 1: Added GI of 53 based on University of Sydney GI Database mung bean vermicelli analogous estimate; calculated GL = 21.2 per 500 g serving.
Iteration 1: Added all three required health_context conditions (Hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, Cardiovascular Disease) with correct machine keys and human_condition_name values.

Iteration 1: Verified aliases_by_language consistency with flat aliases array.
Iteration 2: Corrected ontology.foodon_match_type and foodex2_match_type from 'broader' to 'closest_parent' to comply with allowed values ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none'].

Iteration 3: Added Chinese simplified (清冬阴海鲜粉丝汤), Chinese traditional (清冬陰海鮮粉絲湯), Malay (Sup Tom Yum Makanan Laut Bihun Kaca), Tamil (தெளிவான தாம் யம் கடல் உணவு கண்ணாடி நூடுல்ஸ் சூப்), and Hindi (क्लियर टॉम यम सीफूड ग्लास नूडल सूप) local names to satisfy G6 and Q1/Q8 validator requirements.
Iteration 3: Added all new local-language names verbatim to the flat aliases array and to aliases_by_language to satisfy Q8 consistency check.
Iteration 3: Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' — GI 53 falls within the low GI range (≤ 55) per University of Sydney GI Database classification thresholds.
